fuzzy_prd:
We need a lightweight in-memory data filtering and transformation service that our testing teams can use without standing up any external infrastructure. The core idea is simple: developers should be able to ask the service to scan a list of records and return only those that match a given set of criteria, apply changes to a single record and get the updated version back, or reorder a mixed list of values according to a specified direction. Matching needs to support the usual range checks, set membership tests, structural checks (like whether a field exists at all, or an array has a certain length), pattern-based string matching, nested field access using dotted paths, and combinations of all of the above using logical connectives. Modifications should handle assigning and removing fields, moving values between paths, numeric increments, adding and removing items from lists, and bitwise adjustments to integer fields; for targeting a specific position inside a list, the service should look at the filter that was used to find the record in the first place, similar to how the data-access layer in the auth module figures out which row to lock. When sorting a mixed bag of values that includes numbers, text, booleans, timestamps, identifiers, and the special boundary markers, the service must follow the same cross-type ranking that our document storage layer uses. Invalid or conflicting instructions should produce a concise machine-readable error token rather than an exception trace so downstream tools can handle them cleanly.

A couple extra details from the follow-up questions. For sorting, the fixed ascending order is MinKey < null < numbers (Integer/Long/Double/Float compared numerically) < String < embedded document (BasicDBObject) < array (BasicDBList) < binary/byte[] < ObjectId < Boolean (false before true) < Date < Pattern/regex < MaxKey. Numbers of different kinds (int, long, double, float) are compared by their numeric value using BigDecimal. Also, embedded documents that contain the sort field are placed after all non-document values and ordered by that field's value. If direction is reversed with -1, reverse the entire resulting order, including where those sort-field-bearing documents land. For the boundary markers, MinKey is represented in the JSON wire format as {"$minKey": 1} and sorts before all other types (weight Integer.MIN_VALUE), and MaxKey is represented as {"$maxKey": 1} and sorts after all other types (weight Integer.MAX_VALUE). In sort output they are rendered as {"$minKey": 1} and {"$maxKey": 1} respectively.

On matching, there were a few edge cases worth spelling out. The $ne operator keeps documents where the field is absent (not present in the document at all), but with the same null behavior we use elsewhere: when the stored field path resolves to an empty list (field missing), $ne returns true if and only if the query value is non-null. If the query value is null, an absent field does NOT satisfy $ne. Also, if the stored field is an array and it has at least one element equal to the query value, then $ne returns false. For direct equality when the query value itself is an array, like {"a": [1,2,3]}, a document matches if either the field 'a' is an array equal element-by-element to [1,2,3], or the field 'a' is a non-array value equal to one of the array elements. If the query value is an embedded document, then the stored field has to be an equal document. Equality for typed values like ObjectIds uses value identity.

One other thing the team asked about is how null behaves inside $in and $nin. When a null entry appears in the $in list, documents where the queried field is absent are also matched. Similarly, $nin with a null entry in the list will exclude documents where the field is absent, because absent matches null. That null-matches-absent rule applies to $in and $nin but not to comparison operators like $gte/$lte.

And on updates, if the update spec has no $-prefixed operator keys at all, treat it as a full document replacement. In that case the engine removes all fields from the existing document except _id, then adds all fields from the update specification. The _id field is always preserved from the original document and cannot be replaced.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public DBObject doUpdate(final DBObject obj, final DBObject update, DBObject query, boolean isCreated)

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The $slice modifier in a $push/$each operation works as follows: a non-negative value N keeps the first N elements of the array after appending; a negative value N keeps the last abs(N) elements; a value of zero results in an empty array. $slice operates after $position insertion and before $sort reordering within the same $push operation.

When two modifier operators in the same update specification target the same field path (e.g., both $set and $inc on field 'a'), the engine throws a FongoException with a message containing 'atomic'. The Dispatcher normalizes this to the output line 'error=conflicting_modifiers\n'. This check is enforced via a seenKeys set that rejects duplicate paths across all operators.

The $  positional placeholder in an update path is invalid in two cases: (1) when the path after $ points into a field of an array element but that element is not a DBObject (yields FongoException with 'non-DBObject'); (2) when the path traversal encounters a non-DBObject intermediate node (yields FongoException with 'subfield'). Also, if no matching query key is found for the pre-path, FongoException with 'positional' is thrown. All three cases normalize to 'error=invalid_positional_path\n'.

$exists: true matches any document where the field key is present, regardless of whether its value is null. A document {"a": null} satisfies {"a": {"$exists": true}}. A document {"b": null} does NOT satisfy {"a": {"$exists": true}} because the key 'a' is absent.

$pop with a positive argument (e.g., 1) removes the last element of the array. $pop with a negative argument (e.g., -1) removes the first element. If the array is missing or empty, $pop leaves the document unchanged. The argument value matters only in sign, not magnitude.

Dates are serialized in the output wire format as {"$date": "<ISO-8601 instant>"}, for example {"$date": "1970-01-01T00:00:01.000Z"} for 1000 milliseconds since epoch. The input accepts {"$date": <ms-since-epoch>} as a number, but the output always renders the date as an ISO-8601 UTC instant string with millisecond precision and a 'Z' suffix.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the data-access layer in the auth module figures out which row to lock
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The positional placeholder ($) resolution logic in UpdateEngine.handlePositionalUpdate(), which scans the accompanying query object to find a key matching the pre-path prefix of the update path, builds a Filter from that query sub-expression, and uses it to identify the first matching array index.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the ExpressionParser or UpdateEngine source for the exact behavior, or ask about a specific operator, error code, wire format, or sort ordering rule.

feature1_1_comparison.json.description: A document matcher evaluates a query specification against a stream of documents and returns, in their original order, only the documents that satisfy it. This scenario covers range and equality comparison conditions on a single field: a value strictly or inclusively greater-than or less-than a bound, an explicit equality condition, an inequality (not-equal) condition, ranges combining two bounds, and comparisons against temporal values. When the stored field holds an array, the condition matches if any element satisfies it. A field that is absent or holds the empty/null value does not satisfy a positive comparison.

feature1_2_membership.json.description: A document matcher selects documents whose field relates to a given set of values. The set-membership condition keeps documents whose field equals one of the listed values (or, for an array field, has any element among them); a null entry in the listed values additionally matches documents where the field is absent. The not-in condition is the complement: it keeps documents whose field matches none of the listed values, including documents where the field is absent. The contains-all condition keeps documents whose array field contains every listed value (list entries may themselves be patterns). Matching returns documents in their original order.

feature1_3_element.json.description: A document matcher selects documents based on structural properties of a field rather than its concrete value. The existence condition keeps documents in which the field is present (even when its value is null). The array-size condition keeps documents whose array field has exactly the given number of elements. The modulo condition keeps documents whose numeric field, divided by a given divisor, leaves a given remainder. Documents are returned in their original order; an absent field does not satisfy these positive conditions.

feature1_4_logical.json.description: A document matcher combines conditions with logical connectives. A query object listing several field conditions is an implicit conjunction: every condition must hold. An explicit conjunction connective requires all of its sub-queries to hold. A disjunction connective keeps a document when at least one of its sub-queries holds, and disjunctions may be nested. A negation connective inverts a condition, keeping the documents the inner condition would reject. Matching preserves the original document order.

feature1_5_dotted_path.json.description: A document matcher resolves dotted field paths to reach into nested documents and arrays. A path component may be a sub-document key or a numeric array index. When a path traverses an array of sub-documents without an explicit index, the condition matches if any element along the way satisfies it. Comparison conditions also apply through dotted paths. Documents are returned in their original order; a path that cannot be resolved does not match.

feature1_6_regex.json.description: A document matcher supports regular-expression conditions on string fields. A field condition given as a pattern keeps documents whose string value matches the pattern (anchored as the pattern dictates); when the field is an array of strings, it matches if any element matches. The pattern may be supplied directly or together with option flags (for example, enabling dot-to-match-newline behavior). Regex conditions also apply through dotted paths into nested documents. Non-string and absent values do not match. Documents are returned in their original order.

feature1_7_implicit_equality.json.description: A document matcher treats a plain (non-operator) value as an equality condition. For a scalar this is direct value equality. For an array value, a document matches when its field is an array equal element-by-element to the query array, or when a non-array field equals one of the array's elements. For an embedded-document value, the stored field must equal that document. Equality respects value identity for typed values such as object identifiers. Documents are returned in their original order.

feature2_1_field_modifiers.json.description: An update engine applies a modifier specification to a document and returns the resulting document. A specification with no modifier operators replaces the whole document (the identifier is preserved). The set operator assigns a value at a (possibly nested, dotted) path, creating intermediate documents as needed. The unset operator removes a field at a path. The rename operator moves values from one path to another. The increment operator adds a numeric delta to a field, treating an absent field as zero and preserving the field's numeric kind. Numeric path components address array positions or create numbered sub-keys.

feature2_2_array_append.json.description: An update engine appends to array fields. The push operator adds one element to the end of an array, creating the array when absent. With an each-modifier it appends several elements at once; a slice-modifier then trims the array to a maximum length (keeping the first N for a non-negative N, the last N for a negative N, or empty for zero); a position-modifier inserts the new elements at a given index instead of the end; a sort-modifier reorders the array (by a sub-field ascending/descending, or the whole elements). The push-all operator appends a list of elements. The add-to-set operator appends only values not already present (optionally each of several). Push and increment may be combined in one specification.

feature2_3_array_remove.json.description: An update engine removes elements from array fields. The pop operator drops one element from the array's end (for a positive argument) or its front (for a negative argument), leaving a missing or empty array unchanged. The pull operator removes every element matching a condition: a scalar removes equal elements, and a sub-condition removes matching embedded documents or elements matching a set membership (including pattern membership). The pull-all operator removes every occurrence of any value in a given list.

feature2_4_bitwise.json.description: An update engine applies bitwise modifiers to an integer field. The bit operator can perform a bitwise AND with a given mask, a bitwise OR with a given mask, or both in sequence (AND then OR), replacing the field with the computed integer result.

feature2_5_positional.json.description: An update engine supports the positional placeholder, which targets the first array element matched by the accompanying query. The placeholder may stand for a field inside the matched element, for the matched scalar element itself, or for replacing the whole matched element. The query that located the document (including via an element-match condition) determines which array position the placeholder resolves to; other elements are left untouched.

feature2_6_errors.json.description: An update engine rejects invalid modifier specifications with a normalized error signal instead of a result document. Supplying two conflicting modifier operators that target the same field is reported as a conflicting-modifiers error. Using the positional placeholder against a path that cannot be resolved to an array element of the required shape is reported as an invalid-positional-path error.

feature3_1_type_ordering.json.description: An ordering engine sorts a heterogeneous list of values according to a sort specification (a field with an ascending or descending direction). Values of different BSON kinds are ordered by a fixed inter-type ranking (for example smallest-key, null, numbers, strings, embedded documents, arrays, object identifiers, booleans, dates, regular expressions, largest-key), with numeric values compared across integer/long/double kinds. Embedded documents that contain the sort field are ordered by that field's value. Reversing the direction reverses the resulting order.
